narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who went on to take the bar exam, looking at factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 29 (well under the 36.7 average), Year 1 school decile of 3 (versus an average of 5.71), Year 3 decile of 4 (compared to 5.53), low family income bracket, and plans for full-time work—and predicted a 42% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. The three most influential factors in this decision were your family income, your Year 3 academic decile, and your Year 1 academic decile, each pulling the prediction in different directions. Surprisingly, your low family income was the strongest positive signal, actually pushing the model's assessment toward expecting you to pass the bar, perhaps reflecting resilience often seen in students from similar backgrounds. Your Year 3 decile ranking of 4 worked similarly in your favor, suggesting solid improvement or performance that nudged the odds toward success compared to peers. On the flip side, your Year 1 decile of 3 was the key drag, contributing most directly toward the failure prediction by signaling early academic struggles relative to the typical student. Overall, while your later performance and background offered some counterbalance, the early grades tipped the scale enough to hit that concerning 42% failure threshold. Compared to typical admitted students, your LSAT and GPA are notably lower, which amplified the impact of those early deciles. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it above 35, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters highlighting your perseverance from a low-income background. Many students in your position have succeeded by addressing these gaps—consider community college options or targeted prep courses to build a stronger case for reapplication next cycle. With targeted improvements, you can shift that prediction firmly toward bar exam success.
